attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man is admitted to hospital following a fall. He has a 30-year history of heavy alcohol use and was recently treated for Wernicke encephalopathy. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His parent reports that he cannot remember recent events but tells elaborate, inconsistent stories about what he has been doing.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He is alert and oriented to person but not to place or time. When asked about his activities yesterday, he confidently describes attending his daughter's wedding, though he has no children. When this inconsistency is pointed out, he provides a different, equally detailed account about being at work, despite not having worked for five years.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes this patient's memory disturbance?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of Korsakoff syndrome, characterised by severe anterograde amnesia and confabulation. Confabulation involves the production of fabricated memories to fill gaps in memory, with the patient genuinely believing these false memories to be true. The history of treated Wernicke encephalopathy following chronic alcohol use is typical, as Korsakoff syndrome often develops as a progression of untreated or partially treated Wernicke encephalopathy due to thiamine deficiency.

Delusional memory involves fixed, false beliefs about past events that are held with delusional intensity and are typically part of a broader psychotic illness. Unlike confabulation, these memories are consistent over time and resistant to contradiction.

Dissociative amnesia is typically associated with psychological trauma and involves gaps in autobiographical memory. Unlike confabulation, patients with dissociative amnesia are usually aware of their memory gaps and do not fabricate stories to fill them.

Pseudodementia refers to cognitive impairment that appears to be dementia but is actually due to depression. These patients typically emphasise their memory difficulties and respond 'I don't know' rather than confabulating responses.

Malingering involves the intentional fabrication of symptoms for external gain. Unlike confabulation, malingerers are aware they are providing false information and their stories often lack the spontaneity and conviction seen in confabulation.
learningPoints: Confabulation is a hallmark feature of Korsakoff syndrome where patients fill memory gaps with false memories they believe to be true.
Korsakoff syndrome typically develops in chronic alcohol users with thiamine deficiency following Wernicke encephalopathy.
The combination of anterograde amnesia and confabulation distinguishes Korsakoff syndrome from other causes of memory loss.
references: Wijnia JW. A clinician’s view of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2022 Nov 15;11(22):6755.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
